text: Which enzyme removes the RNA primers during DNA replication in eukaryotes and fills in the resulting gaps?
options: DNA polymerase I
DNA polymerase δ, assisted by RNase H and FEN1 (flap endonuclease 1)
DNA polymerase α
Telomerase
explanation: In eukaryotes, RNA primers are removed by the coordinated action of RNase H1 (degrades RNA in RNA:DNA hybrids) and FEN1 (flap endonuclease 1, cleaves the 5' RNA flap). DNA polymerase δ then fills the gap using the 3'-OH of the upstream Okazaki fragment. DNA ligase I seals the nick. In prokaryotes, DNA pol I has 5'→3' exonuclease activity and performs primer removal itself. DNA pol α synthesizes the initial RNA-DNA primer on each Okazaki fragment. Telomerase extends the 3' overhang of chromosomal ends, not primer removal.

text: A missense mutation in the gene encoding hemoglobin beta chain (HBB) results in a glutamate→valine substitution at position 6. This produces sickle cell hemoglobin (HbS) because:
options: Valine is charged and disrupts the oxygen-binding pocket
Valine is hydrophobic and causes HbS to polymerize under low-oxygen conditions, distorting red blood cell shape
The mutation destroys the heme group
Valine increases oxygen affinity, preventing release to tissues
explanation: The Glu6Val substitution in HBB replaces a charged, hydrophilic residue with a nonpolar, hydrophobic one. Under deoxygenation, the exposed valine on one HbS tetramer fits into a hydrophobic pocket on an adjacent HbS molecule, nucleating polymerization into long fibers. These fibers distort the RBC into the classic sickle shape, increasing viscosity and causing vaso-occlusion. Heterozygotes (HbAS) have sickle-cell trait — usually asymptomatic but protective against falciparum malaria. The heme group is intact; oxygen affinity is actually reduced (right-shifted O2 dissociation curve).

text: During mitosis, the spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC) prevents anaphase onset until:
options: All chromosomes have replicated their DNA
Every kinetochore is properly attached to spindle microtubules (biorientation), satisfying the Mad2/BubR1 checkpoint proteins
The nuclear envelope has completely broken down
Cyclin B is fully degraded
explanation: The SAC (spindle assembly checkpoint) ensures accurate chromosome segregation. Unattached kinetochores catalytically generate a 'wait' signal — they recruit Mad1-Mad2 complexes, which convert cytoplasmic Mad2 into an inhibitory form that sequesters Cdc20, preventing it from activating the APC/C (anaphase-promoting complex/cyclosome). Without active APC/C, securin and cyclin B are not ubiquitinated and degraded, so cohesin is not cleaved by separase, and sister chromatids cannot separate. Once all kinetochores achieve proper amphitelic attachment (biorientation), the 'wait' signal is extinguished, Cdc20 activates APC/C, and anaphase proceeds.

text: Which of the following best describes the role of acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC) in fatty acid synthesis?
options: ACC catalyzes the condensation of two acetyl-CoA molecules to form acetoacetyl-CoA
ACC is the committed, rate-limiting step: it carboxylates acetyl-CoA to malonyl-CoA using biotin and ATP
ACC transfers the malonyl group onto ACP (acyl carrier protein)
ACC performs the final thioesterase step releasing free palmitate
explanation: ACC (acetyl-CoA carboxylase) catalyzes: acetyl-CoA + CO₂ + ATP → malonyl-CoA. This is the committed, rate-limiting step of de novo fatty acid synthesis. It uses biotin as a cofactor (covalently attached). Regulation: activated by citrate (signals surplus acetyl-CoA) and insulin (activates phosphatase that dephosphorylates ACC); inhibited by palmitoyl-CoA (product feedback), glucagon/epinephrine (via AMPK phosphorylation), and malonyl-CoA itself. Malonyl-CoA also inhibits carnitine palmitoyltransferase I, preventing futile cycling between synthesis and β-oxidation.

text: A patient has a deficiency in adenosine deaminase (ADA). Which condition results, and why?
options: Gout, because purine degradation is impaired and uric acid accumulates
Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID), because deoxyadenosine accumulates and is toxic to lymphocytes (especially T cells)
Hemolytic anemia, because RBCs cannot recycle adenosine
Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, because hypoxanthine cannot be salvaged
explanation: ADA converts adenosine → inosine and deoxyadenosine → deoxyinosine. Without ADA, deoxyadenosine accumulates and is phosphorylated to dATP by salvage enzymes. dATP pools are particularly high in lymphocytes, inhibiting ribonucleotide reductase (which requires a balance of dNTPs) and inducing apoptosis via cytochrome c release. T cells are most affected (SCID — 'boy in the bubble' disease). ADA-SCID was the first human disease treated by gene therapy (1990). Lesch-Nyhan involves HGPRT deficiency; gout results from HGPRT or PRPP synthetase mutations.

text: Which statement correctly describes the difference between competitive and uncompetitive enzyme inhibition?
options: Competitive inhibition raises Km and lowers Vmax; uncompetitive inhibition raises Km and raises Vmax
Competitive inhibition raises apparent Km (reducible by adding more substrate); uncompetitive inhibition lowers both apparent Km and Vmax proportionally (Vmax/Km ratio unchanged)
Both types of inhibition increase Km without affecting Vmax
Uncompetitive inhibitors bind the free enzyme; competitive inhibitors bind the enzyme-substrate complex
explanation: Competitive inhibitors bind the free enzyme at the active site (or allosteric site mimicking substrate binding), increasing apparent Km (more substrate needed to outcompete inhibitor) but leaving Vmax unchanged (can be overcome by excess substrate). Uncompetitive inhibitors bind only the ES complex, decreasing both apparent Km and Vmax by the same factor α' — so the Vmax/Km (catalytic efficiency) is unchanged. On a Lineweaver-Burk plot: competitive = same y-intercept, different x-intercept; uncompetitive = parallel lines (same slope); noncompetitive = same x-intercept, different y-intercept.

text: In the liver, glucokinase (hexokinase IV) differs from hexokinase I-III in peripheral tissues primarily because glucokinase:
options: Has a low Km for glucose (~0.1 mM) and is inhibited by its product glucose-6-phosphate
Has a high Km for glucose (~10 mM), is not inhibited by glucose-6-phosphate, and acts as a glucose sensor for the liver and pancreatic β-cells
Is expressed in all tissues and responds to all hexoses equally
Is regulated primarily by AMP concentration rather than glucose concentration
explanation: Glucokinase (GK) features: high Km (~10 mM) — active only when portal blood glucose is high postprandially; sigmoidal kinetics (cooperative); not inhibited by G6P — so liver continues phosphorylating glucose even at high concentrations. This makes GK a glucose sensor: it ramps up glycolysis/glycogen synthesis when glucose is abundant. In contrast, hexokinases I-III have low Km (~0.1 mM), are saturated at normal blood glucose, and are product-inhibited by G6P. Glucokinase is also induced by insulin and expressed in pancreatic β-cells (where it controls insulin secretion threshold).

text: A cell exposed to ultraviolet light develops a thymine dimer. Which DNA repair pathway is primarily responsible for removing this lesion?
options: Base excision repair (BER)
Nucleotide excision repair (NER)
Mismatch repair (MMR)
Homologous recombination (HR)
explanation: UV-induced pyrimidine dimers (cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers and 6-4 photoproducts) cause bulky helix-distorting lesions repaired by NER. NER involves: (1) damage recognition (XPC-RAD23B in global genome NER; stalled RNA pol II in transcription-coupled NER); (2) dual incision ~25 nt around the lesion by ERCC1-XPF and XPG; (3) gap filling by DNA pol δ/ε + RFC + PCNA; (4) ligation. XP (xeroderma pigmentosum) arises from NER defects → extreme UV sensitivity and skin cancer. BER repairs small, non-distorting lesions (oxidized/deaminated bases). MMR corrects replication errors. HR repairs double-strand breaks.

text: The complement system can be activated by three pathways. Which pathway is activated first during a primary infection with a novel bacterium before antibodies are produced?
options: Classical pathway, via IgM/IgG antibody-antigen complexes activating C1q
Lectin pathway (via MBL binding to bacterial carbohydrates) and alternative pathway (spontaneous C3 hydrolysis), both of which are antibody-independent
Terminal pathway, directly forming the membrane attack complex
Only the classical pathway operates during infection
explanation: The alternative pathway (AP) is constitutively active at low levels via spontaneous C3 hydrolysis ('tick-over'). When C3b deposits on a bacterial surface, it amplifies rapidly (properdin stabilizes the C3bBb convertase). The lectin pathway activates when mannose-binding lectin (MBL) or ficolins bind carbohydrate patterns on bacteria → MASP-1/2 cleave C4 and C2. Both pathways are antibody-independent and constitute innate immunity. The classical pathway requires IgM or IgG antibodies (adaptive immunity, requires prior exposure or vaccination). All three converge at C3 cleavage → C3a/C3b → C5 cleavage → membrane attack complex (C5b-9).

text: In the fed state, insulin signaling activates which intracellular pathway to promote glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue?
options: cAMP-PKA pathway, which phosphorylates GLUT4 vesicles
PI3K-Akt pathway, which causes GLUT4-containing vesicles to translocate from intracellular stores to the plasma membrane
JAK-STAT pathway, which transcribes the GLUT4 gene
MAPK pathway, which activates glycogen synthase directly
explanation: Insulin binds its receptor tyrosine kinase → autophosphorylation → phosphorylation of IRS-1/2 → recruits PI3K → produces PIP3 → activates PDK1 → phosphorylates and activates Akt (PKB). Akt phosphorylates AS160 (a RabGAP), relieving inhibition of Rab proteins on GLUT4 vesicles → GLUT4 storage vesicles fuse with the plasma membrane → increased glucose import. Insulin also activates glycogen synthase (via PP1, inactivating GSK3). In type 2 diabetes, PI3K-Akt signaling is impaired (insulin resistance). Exercise also causes GLUT4 translocation via AMPK independently of insulin.

text: Which vitamin deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia by the same mechanism as folate deficiency, but also causes irreversible neurological damage?
options: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine)
Vitamin B12 (cobalamin)
Vitamin C (ascorbic acid)
Vitamin B1 (thiamine)
explanation: B12 (cobalamin) deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia because B12 is required for methionine synthase (converts homocysteine → methionine, simultaneously converting methylTHF → THF). Without B12, folate is 'trapped' as methylTHF and unavailable for thymidylate synthesis → impaired DNA synthesis → large, immature RBCs (megaloblasts). Importantly, B12 is also required for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (converts methylmalonyl-CoA → succinyl-CoA). Without this, methylmalonyl-CoA accumulates → neurological damage (subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord: posterior and lateral columns). Folate deficiency causes identical anemia but NO neurological damage. Pernicious anemia: autoimmune loss of intrinsic factor → B12 malabsorption.

text: A researcher observes that a signaling protein requires phosphorylation on both a tyrosine and a serine residue for full activation. Phosphorylation of either residue alone produces only partial activity. This is an example of:
options: Allosteric inhibition
Post-translational modification combinatorial control — multiple phosphorylation events integrate to produce a graded or threshold response
Feedback inhibition
Transcriptional regulation
explanation: Many signaling proteins require multiple post-translational modifications (PTMs) for full activity, allowing combinatorial integration of signals. This is sometimes called a 'coincidence detection' mechanism: full activation only when both an upstream tyrosine kinase and a serine/threonine kinase are active simultaneously. Examples: ERK requires dual phosphorylation on Thr and Tyr by MEK; Akt requires phosphorylation by PDK1 (Thr308) and mTORC2 (Ser473) for maximal activity. This architecture creates bistability, noise filtering, and signal integration — enabling cells to respond only when multiple conditions are met simultaneously.

text: During embryonic development, the notochord induces overlying ectoderm to form the neural plate via a process called primary neurulation. The key molecular mechanism involves:
options: Wnt signaling from the notochord activating neural fate in all ectoderm
Secretion of BMP antagonists (Chordin, Noggin, Follistatin) by the notochord and dorsal mesoderm, inhibiting BMP4 and allowing neural fate in dorsal ectoderm
FGF signaling that converts mesoderm into neural tissue
Sonic hedgehog from the notochord directly converting ectoderm to neurons
explanation: Default model of neural induction: BMP4 (from ventral ectoderm and lateral mesoderm) promotes epidermal fate. The Spemann organizer (dorsal blastopore lip in amphibians) secretes BMP antagonists — Chordin, Noggin, and Follistatin — which bind and block BMP4. In dorsal ectoderm, where BMP activity is low, cells adopt neural plate fate (default). Lateral ectoderm (high BMP4) becomes epidermis. Sonic hedgehog from the notochord ventralizes the neural tube (floor plate, motor neurons). This 'morphogen gradient' model explains dorsoventral patterning of the neural tube.

text: Which of the following correctly describes the mechanism of CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing?
options: Cas9 is an RNA polymerase that transcribes corrected sequences into the genome

Cas9 is an RNA-guided endonuclease: a single guide RNA (sgRNA) directs Cas9 to a complementary genomic sequence adjacent to a PAM site, where Cas9 makes a double-strand break that can be repaired by NHEJ or HDR
Cas9 methylates target DNA to silence gene expression
CRISPR works by inserting transposable elements adjacent to target genes
explanation: CRISPR-Cas9 mechanism: (1) Design a 20-nt guide RNA sequence complementary to the target DNA; (2) sgRNA (guide RNA + scaffold) directs Cas9 to the target, which must be adjacent to a PAM sequence (NGG for SpCas9); (3) Cas9 unwinds DNA, checks for sgRNA complementarity, and cleaves both strands (RuvC domain cuts non-complementary strand; HNH domain cuts complementary strand) → blunt DSB; (4) Repair: NHEJ causes insertions/deletions (knockouts); HDR with donor template allows precise edits. Base editors (CBE, ABE) and prime editors extend the toolkit without DSBs. CRISPRi uses dead Cas9 (dCas9) fused to repressors for gene silencing.

text: A patient with galactosemia cannot convert galactose-1-phosphate to glucose-1-phosphate. Which enzyme is deficient?
options: Galactokinase
Galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase (GALT)
UDP-galactose 4-epimerase
Lactase
explanation: Classic galactosemia is caused by deficiency of galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase (GALT), which catalyzes: Gal-1-P + UDP-glucose ↔ Glc-1-P + UDP-galactose. Without GALT, Gal-1-P accumulates → toxic to liver, brain, and kidneys. Presentation: jaundice, cataracts (galactitol via aldose reductase), intellectual disability, E. coli sepsis in neonates. Treatment: lactose-free diet. Galactokinase deficiency: milder — only cataracts (galactitol accumulation). UDP-galactose 4-epimerase deficiency: rarest form. Lactase deficiency causes lactose intolerance (GI symptoms only, no systemic toxicity).

text: The Na+/K+-ATPase pump transports 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in per ATP hydrolyzed. How does this contribute to the resting membrane potential?
options: It has no effect on membrane potential because both ions are transported

It contributes directly to resting membrane potential by making the inside of the cell more negative (electrogenic pump), and critically maintains Na+ and K+ concentration gradients that drive K+ leak channels to establish the resting potential
It depolarizes the cell by bringing K+ inside
The pump only maintains osmotic balance and has no electrical effect
explanation: The Na+/K+-ATPase is electrogenic: net export of 1 positive charge per cycle (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in) directly hyperpolarizes the membrane by ~3-5 mV. More importantly, it maintains the concentration gradients (high [K+] inside, high [Na+] outside) that drive K+ leak channels. K+ efflux through leak channels (down its concentration gradient) is the primary determinant of resting membrane potential (~-70 mV in neurons), as described by the Goldman equation. Without the pump, gradients would dissipate and resting potential would collapse. The pump is inhibited by cardiac glycosides (digoxin, ouabain) → indirectly increases intracellular Ca²+ → positive inotropy.

text: Which of the following correctly describes the genetic basis of X-linked recessive inheritance?
options: Affected fathers always pass the disease to all daughters

Males are affected when they carry one mutant X allele (hemizygous); females are carriers with one mutant allele and are usually unaffected; affected fathers pass the carrier state to all daughters but not to sons
The condition skips generations in females but never skips in males
Homozygous females are less severely affected than hemizygous males
explanation: X-linked recessive (e.g., Duchenne MD, hemophilia A/B, G6PD deficiency, color blindness): Males (XY) are hemizygous — one mutant X allele causes disease. Females (XX) need two mutant alleles to be affected (usually carriers). Key inheritance rules: (1) Affected father × unaffected mother → all daughters are carriers, all sons unaffected (fathers pass X only to daughters); (2) Carrier mother × unaffected father → 50% of sons affected, 50% of daughters carriers; (3) No father-to-son transmission (sons get Y from father). Lyonization (X-inactivation) can cause mild symptoms in carriers. Affected daughters require an affected father AND carrier/affected mother.

text: A bacterium exposed to a beta-lactam antibiotic develops resistance by producing beta-lactamase. This enzyme:
options: Modifies the bacterial ribosome so the antibiotic cannot bind
Hydrolyzes the beta-lactam ring, inactivating the antibiotic before it can inhibit transpeptidase (PBP) and disrupt cell wall synthesis
Actively pumps the antibiotic out of the bacterial cell
Prevents the antibiotic from crossing the outer membrane
explanation: Beta-lactam antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems) inhibit transpeptidases (penicillin-binding proteins, PBPs) that cross-link peptidoglycan strands in the bacterial cell wall. Beta-lactamases are serine hydrolases that open the beta-lactam ring → inactivated antibiotic. Beta-lactamase genes are often on plasmids and rapidly spread by horizontal gene transfer. Clinical strategies to overcome beta-lactamase: (1) beta-lactamase inhibitors (clavulanate, sulbactam, tazobactam) combined with a beta-lactam; (2) Carbapenems resist most common beta-lactamases (but carbapenemases — KPC, NDM — now a major concern). MRSA resistance instead involves altered PBP2a with reduced affinity.

text: During the citric acid cycle, which step directly produces GTP (or ATP) by substrate-level phosphorylation?
options: Isocitrate → alpha-ketoglutarate (catalyzed by isocitrate dehydrogenase)
Succinyl-CoA → succinate (catalyzed by succinyl-CoA synthetase)
Fumarate → malate (catalyzed by fumarase)
Oxaloacetate → citrate (catalyzed by citrate synthase)
explanation: Succinyl-CoA synthetase (also called succinate thiokinase) couples the thioester bond hydrolysis of succinyl-CoA to substrate-level phosphorylation: succinyl-CoA + GDP (or ADP) + Pi → succinate + GTP (or ATP) + CoA. This is the only substrate-level phosphorylation step in the TCA cycle. The complete TCA cycle (per acetyl-CoA turn) yields: 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, 1 GTP, 2 CO2. The other steps produce NADH (isocitrate DH, α-ketoglutarate DH, malate DH) or FADH2 (succinate DH). Citrate synthase and fumarase catalyze condensation and hydration reactions respectively — no phosphorylation.

text: The HIV virus packages two copies of its RNA genome along with reverse transcriptase. After infecting a CD4+ T cell, the first product of reverse transcriptase activity is:
options: Double-stranded DNA immediately
A DNA-RNA hybrid (negative-sense DNA strand synthesized from the viral RNA template)
A positive-sense RNA copy used directly for translation
Integrated proviral DNA without any RNA intermediate
explanation: HIV reverse transcriptase (RT) has three activities: RNA-dependent DNA polymerase, RNase H, and DNA-dependent DNA polymerase. Steps: (1) RT uses a host tRNA as primer and the viral (+)RNA genome as template → synthesizes (-)DNA strand = DNA-RNA hybrid; (2) RNase H degrades the RNA component of the hybrid; (3) RT synthesizes the second (+)DNA strand using the (-)DNA as template → double-stranded DNA (dsDNA); (4) Integrase imports dsDNA into the nucleus and integrates it into the host chromosome (provirus). RT lacks proofreading → high mutation rate → rapid emergence of drug resistance. NRTI/NNRTIs target RT; integrase strand transfer inhibitors (INSTIs) target integrase.

text: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disease caused by antibodies against nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChR) at the neuromuscular junction. The primary symptom—muscle weakness that worsens with use—occurs because:
options: Autoantibodies block acetylcholinesterase, causing excessive ACh stimulation

Autoantibodies block, internalize, and trigger complement-mediated destruction of nAChRs at the motor end plate, reducing the safety factor for neuromuscular transmission; repeated stimulation depletes available receptors faster than they can be replaced
Autoantibodies prevent ACh synthesis in motor nerve terminals
Autoantibodies activate nAChRs constitutively, causing fatigue
explanation: In MG, IgG antibodies against nAChR (primarily the α-subunit) cause: (1) direct blockade of ACh binding; (2) crosslinking and accelerated internalization/degradation of receptors; (3) complement-mediated destruction of the post-synaptic membrane. The end plate potential (EPP) is reduced below threshold → failure of muscle fiber recruitment ('decremental response' on EMG). Weakness worsens with repeated use because the safety margin is already compromised; repetitive stimulation further depletes ACh release. Treatment: acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (neostigmine, pyridostigmine) increase synaptic ACh; immunosuppression; thymectomy (thymic hyperplasia/thymoma is often associated). Lambert-Eaton: autoantibodies against voltage-gated Ca²+ channels on presynaptic terminal → reduced ACh release; weakness improves with repeated stimulation.

text: Which of the following correctly describes the mechanism of action of tamoxifen in treating estrogen receptor-positive (ER+) breast cancer?
options: Tamoxifen is a cytotoxic agent that directly kills rapidly dividing cells
Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that competitively binds ERα in breast tissue, acting as an antagonist to block estrogen-driven transcription of proliferative genes
Tamoxifen inhibits aromatase, reducing peripheral estrogen synthesis
Tamoxifen is a monoclonal antibody targeting HER2/neu
explanation: Tamoxifen is a SERM (selective estrogen receptor modulator): it competitively binds ERα with high affinity. In breast tissue, the tamoxifen-ER complex recruits co-repressors (rather than co-activators) → blocks transcription of estrogen-responsive genes (cyclin D1, MYC) → antiproliferative. In bone and uterus, the same complex acts as a partial agonist (explains bone protection but increased endometrial cancer risk). Tamoxifen undergoes hepatic metabolism to its active form (endoxifen) via CYP2D6 — CYP2D6 poor metabolizers have reduced benefit. Aromatase inhibitors (letrozole, anastrozole) reduce estrogen synthesis — used in postmenopausal women. Trastuzumab targets HER2.

text: The conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA by the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) is irreversible and requires multiple cofactors. Which of the following cofactors is NOT required by PDC?
options: Thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP)
Lipoic acid
FAD
Biotin
explanation: PDC is a multi-enzyme complex: E1 (pyruvate decarboxylase) uses TPP; E2 (dihydrolipoyl transacetylase) uses lipoic acid and CoA; E3 (dihydrolipoyl dehydrogenase) uses FAD and NAD+. The reaction: pyruvate + CoA + NAD+ → acetyl-CoA + CO₂ + NADH. Biotin is NOT a PDC cofactor — it is used by carboxylases (pyruvate carboxylase, acetyl-CoA carboxylase, propionyl-CoA carboxylase). PDC is inhibited by acetyl-CoA and NADH (products) and by PDC kinase (phosphorylation). PDC kinase is activated by high ATP/ADP and acetyl-CoA/CoA ratios. PDC deficiency causes lactic acidosis and neurological damage — pyruvate cannot enter TCA, accumulates, and is converted to lactate.

text: In an autosomal dominant condition with 80% penetrance, a heterozygous individual has what probability of showing the phenotype?
explanation: Penetrance is the proportion of individuals with a given genotype who express the associated phenotype. With 80% penetrance, 80% of heterozygous individuals (who carry the dominant allele) will manifest the condition. The remaining 20% carry the allele but are phenotypically unaffected ('non-penetrant'). This differs from expressivity, which describes the degree/severity of phenotypic expression among those who do show it. Examples of variable penetrance: BRCA1/2 mutations (high but not 100% penetrance for breast cancer), retinoblastoma, neurofibromatosis. Penetrance affects apparent inheritance patterns — reduced penetrance can cause autosomal dominant conditions to appear to 'skip' generations.

text: Which lysosomal storage disease results from deficiency of glucocerebrosidase (acid β-glucosidase), and what accumulates?
options: Tay-Sachs disease; GM2 ganglioside accumulates in neurons
Gaucher disease; glucocerebroside (glucosylceramide) accumulates in macrophages of the liver, spleen, and bone marrow
Niemann-Pick disease; sphingomyelin accumulates in lysosomes
Fabry disease; globotriaosylceramide accumulates in vascular endothelium
explanation: Gaucher disease (most common lysosomal storage disease): deficiency of glucocerebrosidase (GBA gene) → accumulation of glucosylceramide in macrophages → 'Gaucher cells' (lipid-laden macrophages with crinkled tissue-paper cytoplasm) in liver, spleen, and bone marrow. Findings: hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia (bone marrow infiltration), bone pain/fractures, osteonecrosis. Type 1 (non-neuronopathic): most common, especially in Ashkenazi Jews. Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy (imiglucerase) or substrate reduction therapy (miglustat). Tay-Sachs: hexosaminidase A deficiency → GM2 ganglioside in neurons. Niemann-Pick: sphingomyelinase deficiency. Fabry: α-galactosidase A deficiency.

text: During clonal selection of B cells, somatic hypermutation occurs in which structure, and what is its purpose?
options: In the bone marrow during VDJ recombination, to generate initial diversity
In the germinal center of secondary lymphoid organs, to introduce point mutations in immunoglobulin variable regions, enabling affinity maturation (selection for higher-affinity antibodies)
In the thymus, to diversify T cell receptors
In the spleen, to switch antibody isotype from IgM to IgG
explanation: After B cell activation by antigen + T helper cell help, B cells migrate into germinal centers (GCs) of lymph nodes/spleen. In GCs: (1) Somatic hypermutation (SHM) — AID (activation-induced cytidine deaminase) introduces point mutations throughout the variable regions of immunoglobulin genes at ~10^6× normal mutation rate; (2) Affinity maturation — B cells with higher-affinity BCRs receive survival signals from follicular dendritic cells (FDCs) presenting antigen; low-affinity clones undergo apoptosis; (3) Class switch recombination (CSR) — also AID-dependent, switches from IgM to IgG, IgA, or IgE. The result: memory B cells and plasma cells producing high-affinity antibodies of diverse isotypes.

text: In the kidney, which segment of the nephron reabsorbs the majority of filtered glucose via sodium-glucose cotransporters (SGLTs)?
options: Thin descending limb of the loop of Henle
Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT), via SGLT2 on the apical membrane coupled with GLUT2 on the basolateral side
Distal convoluted tubule (DCT)
Collecting duct
explanation: Nearly all filtered glucose (~99.9%) is reabsorbed in the proximal tubule: ~90% in the S1/S2 segments by SGLT2 (low affinity, high capacity) and ~10% in S3 by SGLT1 (high affinity, low capacity). SGLT2 uses the Na+ electrochemical gradient (maintained by Na+/K+-ATPase on the basolateral membrane) to co-transport glucose against its concentration gradient into the cell; GLUT2 then facilitates glucose efflux into the interstitium. The renal threshold for glucose is ~180 mg/dL — above this, transport maximum (Tm) is exceeded and glucose spills into urine (glycosuria). SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) are used in T2DM to promote urinary glucose excretion, lowering blood glucose and providing cardiorenal benefits.

text: Which molecular mechanism explains the cooperative oxygen binding of hemoglobin?
options: Each subunit of hemoglobin binds oxygen independently with the same affinity
Oxygen binding to one subunit induces a conformational change (T→R state transition) that increases oxygen affinity in the remaining subunits — a sigmoidal O2 dissociation curve results
Hemoglobin has only one oxygen binding site
Cooperative binding is due to allosteric inhibition by 2,3-BPG at all four subunits simultaneously
explanation: Hemoglobin (α2β2 tetramer) exhibits positive cooperativity due to conformational coupling between subunits. Deoxy-Hb is in the T (tense, low-affinity) state — stabilized by salt bridges and 2,3-BPG. Binding of O2 to one heme iron pulls the iron into the porphyrin plane → moves the proximal histidine → shifts the alpha-helix → disrupts T-state contacts → shifts equilibrium toward R (relaxed, high-affinity) state → remaining subunits bind O2 more easily. The result: sigmoidal O2 dissociation curve (vs. hyperbolic for myoglobin). Hill coefficient ~2.7 (theoretical max = 4). Modulators of O2 affinity: 2,3-BPG, CO2, H+, and temperature all stabilize T state (right shift = Bohr effect).

text: A patient with a liver tumor has elevated serum AFP (alpha-fetoprotein). AFP is an example of:
options: A tumor suppressor protein whose loss promotes cancer
A tumor marker — a fetal protein normally present during embryogenesis (synthesized by fetal liver and yolk sac) that is re-expressed in hepatocellular carcinoma and nonseminomatous germ cell tumors
A hormone secreted by the pituitary in response to liver disease
An immune checkpoint protein that inhibits T cell killing of tumor cells
explanation: AFP is normally produced by the fetal liver and yolk sac and is the major serum protein in the fetus (analogous to albumin in adults). AFP levels fall sharply after birth and are very low in adults. Re-expression of AFP indicates dedifferentiation (cancer cells reverting to fetal gene expression patterns). Elevated serum AFP is a marker for: hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), yolk sac tumors (a type of nonseminomatous germ cell tumor), and some mixed germ cell tumors. AFP is also elevated in neural tube defects (elevated maternal serum AFP) and hepatitis. PSA is a prostate cancer marker; CEA for colon cancer; CA-125 for ovarian cancer; hCG for trophoblastic tumors and germ cell tumors.

text: The enzyme ribonucleotide reductase (RNR) converts ribonucleotides to deoxyribonucleotides. Why is this enzyme a target for anticancer drugs?
options: It is the rate-limiting step of RNA synthesis

RNR is essential for de novo deoxyribonucleotide synthesis required for DNA replication; inhibiting it selectively kills rapidly dividing cancer cells that depend on de novo synthesis rather than salvage pathways
It catalyzes the methylation of deoxycytidine to 5-methylcytosine
It activates nucleoside analogs by phosphorylation
explanation: RNR (class I in mammals) reduces ribonucleoside diphosphates (ADP, CDP, GDP, UDP) to the corresponding deoxyribonucleoside diphosphates using a tyrosyl radical and thioredoxin as electron donor. This is the only de novo pathway for dNTP synthesis, making it essential for S-phase DNA replication. Allosteric regulation: overall activity regulated by ATP/dATP; substrate specificity regulated by binding of specific dNTPs at the specificity site. Cancer cells proliferate rapidly and need large dNTP pools. Hydroxyurea inhibits RNR by quenching the tyrosyl radical — used in CML, sickle cell disease (increases HbF), and myeloproliferative disorders. Gemcitabine (also inhibits RNR after incorporation) is used in pancreatic cancer.

text: Which of the following correctly describes the function of MHC class II molecules?
options: MHC II presents endogenous (intracellular) peptides to CD8+ T cells on all nucleated cells

MHC II presents exogenous peptides (from extracellular proteins degraded in endosomes/lysosomes) to CD4+ T helper cells; expression is restricted to professional antigen-presenting cells (dendritic cells, macrophages, B cells)
MHC II presents lipid antigens via CD1 molecules to NKT cells
MHC II is expressed on all cells and presents viral peptides during active infection
explanation: MHC class II pathway: professional APCs (dendritic cells, macrophages, B cells) engulf extracellular proteins → endosome → lysosomal proteases degrade protein → peptides (13-25 aa) loaded onto MHC II (replacing CLIP via HLA-DM) → MHC II-peptide complex displayed on surface → CD4+ T helper cells recognize this complex (their TCR + CD4 co-receptor binds MHC II). Activated Th cells then help B cells (antibody production) or macrophages (cytotoxicity). MHC I presents endogenous peptides (8-10 aa, from proteasome → TAP → ER) to CD8+ CTLs on all nucleated cells. CD1 presents lipid antigens to NKT cells.

text: In the context of the blood-brain barrier (BBB), which statement best explains why most small molecule drugs can cross the BBB while most large molecules cannot?
options: Large molecules are actively transported out of the CNS by ABC transporters only

The BBB consists of brain capillary endothelial cells joined by tight junctions. Passage depends primarily on lipophilicity and molecular size: small, lipophilic, uncharged molecules diffuse passively; large or polar molecules require specific transporters. Most large biologics (antibodies, proteins) cannot cross.
The BBB selectively allows all nutrients to pass but blocks all drugs regardless of size
Water-soluble molecules cross the BBB faster than lipid-soluble molecules
explanation: The BBB is formed by: tight junctions (claudins, occludins) between brain capillary endothelial cells (eliminating paracellular transport), pericytes, and astrocyte end-feet. Key crossing determinants: (1) Lipophilicity — lipophilic drugs partition into cell membranes and diffuse transcellularly; (2) Molecular weight — ideally <500 Da (Lipinski's rule of five); (3) Efflux transporters — P-glycoprotein (ABCB1) and BCRP pump many drugs back out; (4) Specific carriers — GLUT1 for glucose, LAT1 for large neutral amino acids. Large biologics (mAbs, proteins) cannot cross without modifications (e.g., receptor-mediated transcytosis strategies). This explains why CNS drug development is particularly challenging.

text: During fasting, the liver produces ketone bodies. Which enzyme catalyzes the rate-limiting step of ketogenesis, and in which organelle does ketogenesis occur?
options: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase in the cytoplasm
HMG-CoA synthase in the mitochondrial matrix; ketogenesis occurs entirely in the mitochondria of hepatocytes
HMG-CoA reductase in the smooth ER
Beta-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase in the cytoplasm
explanation: Ketogenesis occurs in the liver mitochondrial matrix (hepatocytes have high β-oxidation activity, producing excess acetyl-CoA that exceeds TCA cycle capacity when oxaloacetate is low during fasting). Steps: 2 acetyl-CoA → acetoacetyl-CoA (thiolase) → HMG-CoA (HMG-CoA synthase, rate-limiting) → acetoacetate + acetyl-CoA (HMG-CoA lyase) → β-hydroxybutyrate (β-HB dehydrogenase, reduced form exported predominantly). Ketone bodies (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate, acetone) are exported to blood and oxidized in extrahepatic tissues (brain, muscle, heart) — liver lacks succinyl-CoA transferase (thiophorase) needed to activate them. DKA occurs when insulin deficiency causes uncontrolled ketogenesis.

text: Which of the following is the correct mechanism of action of aminoglycoside antibiotics (e.g., gentamicin, streptomycin)?
options: They inhibit bacterial cell wall synthesis by blocking transpeptidase
They bind the 30S ribosomal subunit, causing misreading of mRNA codons (mistranslation) and blocking translocation, inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis
They inhibit bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV
They intercalate into bacterial DNA and prevent transcription
explanation: Aminoglycosides are bactericidal antibiotics that: (1) Enter bacteria via oxygen-dependent uptake (ineffective against anaerobes); (2) Bind the 16S rRNA of the 30S ribosomal subunit, stabilizing the ribosome in a 'ram' (ribosomal ambiguity) state → misreading of mRNA codons → incorporation of wrong amino acids; (3) Also block translocation, trapping the ribosome on mRNA. Mistranslated proteins insert into the cell membrane, increasing permeability → more drug enters → irreversible, concentration-dependent bactericidal effect. Resistance: enzymatic inactivation (acetyltransferases, nucleotidyltransferases, phosphotransferases), reduced uptake, 16S rRNA methylation. Toxicities: nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity (vestibular and cochlear).

text: In flow cytometry, forward scatter (FSC) and side scatter (SSC) are used to characterize cells. What do these measurements indicate?
options: FSC measures cell fluorescence intensity; SSC measures cell size
FSC correlates with cell size/volume; SSC correlates with internal complexity/granularity (organelles, nuclear structure)
Both FSC and SSC measure DNA content for cell cycle analysis
FSC measures cell viability; SSC measures membrane integrity
explanation: Flow cytometry scatter parameters: Forward scatter (FSC) is light scattered at small angles (~2-10°) in the direction of the laser beam — proportional to cell size/volume (larger cells scatter more light forward). Side scatter (SSC) is light scattered at 90° — proportional to internal complexity, granularity, and membrane roughness (cells with more granules, organelles, or nuclear lobulations scatter more light sideways). Classic example: a FSC vs. SSC dot plot of blood distinguishes lymphocytes (small, low FSC; agranular, low SSC), monocytes (medium FSC, medium SSC), and granulocytes (large FSC, high SSC). Fluorescent antibodies (detected in specific channels) identify specific surface markers (CD4, CD8, etc.).

text: A patient presents with jaundice and pale stools. Laboratory results show elevated direct (conjugated) bilirubin with normal indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin. This pattern is most consistent with:
options: Pre-hepatic (hemolytic) jaundice due to massive RBC destruction
Post-hepatic (obstructive) jaundice — bile duct obstruction prevents conjugated bilirubin from entering the duodenum; it regurgitates into blood. Pale stools result from absent urobilinogen
Neonatal jaundice due to immature hepatic UGT1A1
Gilbert syndrome due to mildly reduced UGT1A1 activity
explanation: Bilirubin metabolism: unconjugated bilirubin (indirect) → liver → conjugated to glucuronate by UGT1A1 → conjugated bilirubin (direct) → bile → duodenum → urobilinogen → stool (stercobilin = brown color) / urine (urobilin). Pre-hepatic: ↑indirect (excess production exceeds conjugation); hemolytic anemia, neonatal jaundice. Hepatic: ↑both (mixed); hepatitis, cirrhosis, Gilbert (↑indirect only). Post-hepatic: ↑direct (conjugated bilirubin regurgitates because bile duct is obstructed); gallstones (choledocholithiasis), pancreatic head cancer, cholangiocarcinoma. Pale/clay stools = no bile reaching intestine. Dark urine = conjugated bilirubin in urine (water-soluble). Pruritus from bile salts.

text: The lac operon's promoter has two regulatory regions: the operator and the CAP binding site. Which combination results in maximal transcription of the lac operon?
options: High glucose AND lactose present
Low glucose AND lactose present — low glucose raises cAMP activating CAP, and lactose removes the repressor
Low glucose AND NO lactose present
High glucose AND NO lactose present
explanation: Maximal lac operon transcription requires both control mechanisms to favor transcription simultaneously: (1) Negative control OFF — lactose (as allolactose) must be present to inactivate the lac repressor, allowing RNA pol access to the promoter; (2) Positive control ON — glucose must be absent/low so adenylyl cyclase produces cAMP, which binds CAP (catabolite activator protein), and the CAP-cAMP complex binds upstream of the promoter, enhancing RNA pol recruitment ~50-fold. E. coli prefers glucose; when glucose is absent and lactose is available, the cell maximally expresses lac genes to use lactose as an alternative energy source. If glucose is present (even with lactose), CAP is inactive → weak transcription despite repressor removal.

text: A protein has a pI of 8.0. In a solution at pH 7.0, the protein carries a net:
options: Negative charge, and migrates toward the cathode in electrophoresis
Positive charge, and migrates toward the anode (negative electrode) in electrophoresis
No charge; it is at its isoelectric point
Positive charge, and migrates toward the cathode (negative electrode) — but wait, toward the positive electrode (anode)
explanation: At pH below its pI, a protein is net positively charged (more protonated). At pH above its pI, it is net negatively charged. Here, pH 7.0 < pI 8.0, so the protein carries a net positive charge. In gel electrophoresis at pH 7.0, positively charged proteins migrate toward the cathode (negative electrode) — but in standard SDS-PAGE, SDS masks all charges. In native gel electrophoresis or isoelectric focusing, the protein at pH 7.0 < pI 8.0 would carry positive charge and migrate toward the negative electrode (cathode). The correct answer: net positive charge. In gel electrophoresis toward the anode (positive) is incorrect — positively charged molecules move toward the negative electrode (cathode). So the answer is: positive charge, migrates toward the cathode.

text: Which of the following best explains why cancer cells frequently upregulate aerobic glycolysis (Warburg effect) even in the presence of oxygen?
options: Cancer cells lack functional mitochondria and cannot perform oxidative phosphorylation

Aerobic glycolysis provides biosynthetic precursors (ribose-5-phosphate, NADPH, amino acid precursors, lipid building blocks) for rapid cell growth; the rapid (though less efficient) ATP production also supports proliferating cells
Aerobic glycolysis generates more ATP per glucose molecule than oxidative phosphorylation
Glycolysis is less efficient but cancer cells have unlimited glucose supply
explanation: The Warburg effect (aerobic glycolysis) occurs in most proliferating cancer cells: even with oxygen, they preferentially convert glucose to lactate. Key reasons: (1) Biosynthetic advantage — glycolytic intermediates (G6P → pentose phosphate pathway for nucleotides; pyruvate/acetyl-CoA for fatty acids/amino acids; DHAP for glycerol) are needed for building new cells; (2) Rapid ATP — despite lower yield (~2 vs. ~30 ATP/glucose), glycolysis is ~100× faster than OXPHOS; (3) NADPH production via PPP for biosynthesis and antioxidant defense; (4) Acidic tumor microenvironment from lactate secretion suppresses immune cells. Mutations in metabolic enzymes (IDH1/2) and Myc/HIF-1α transcription factors promote the Warburg effect. PET scans exploit high glucose uptake in tumors ([18F]-FDG).

text: Which statement correctly describes the function of tight junctions in epithelial cells?
options: Tight junctions provide mechanical strength by linking the cytoskeletons of adjacent cells via cadherins

Tight junctions form a paracellular seal between adjacent epithelial cells, preventing passive diffusion of molecules and ions between cells, and creating distinct apical and basolateral membrane domains (cell polarity)
Tight junctions facilitate communication between adjacent cells via gap junctions
Tight junctions attach epithelial cells to the basement membrane
explanation: Tight junctions (zonula occludens) are the most apical of the junctional complex. They consist of transmembrane proteins (claudins, occludins, JAM) that form 'kissing' contacts between adjacent cells, creating: (1) A paracellular barrier — prevents free movement of ions and molecules between epithelial cells (the 'gate' function); tightness varies by tissue (kidney proximal tubule = 'leaky'; bladder = 'tight'); (2) A fence function — maintains membrane polarity by preventing mixing of apical and basolateral lipid/protein domains. Desmosomes (cadherins + intermediate filaments) provide mechanical strength. Gap junctions (connexins) allow cytoplasmic communication. Hemidesmosomes/focal adhesions connect cells to basement membrane.

text: A researcher finds that a drug inhibits the enzyme thymidylate synthase. What is the expected downstream effect of this inhibition?
options: Impaired synthesis of ATP and GTP due to reduced purine nucleotide production
Impaired DNA synthesis due to depletion of dTMP (thymidylate) — cells cannot form the thymidine triphosphate (dTTP) needed to replicate DNA; uracil is misincorporated instead
Impaired RNA synthesis due to reduced UMP availability
Increased methylation of DNA
explanation: Thymidylate synthase (TS) catalyzes: dUMP + methyleneTHF → dTMP + DHF. This is the only de novo source of dTMP (thymidylate), which is phosphorylated to dTDP → dTTP for DNA synthesis. TS inhibition → dTTP depletion → inability to replicate DNA (thymineless death). The methylene-THF is oxidized to DHF → DHFR must regenerate it to THF. 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) works by being converted to 5-fluoro-dUMP (FdUMP), a potent irreversible TS inhibitor (suicide inhibition, with methyleneTHF stabilizing the covalent ternary complex). Methotrexate inhibits DHFR, also depleting THF and thus blocking TS indirectly. Both are used in cancer chemotherapy. Leucovorin (folinic acid) is the antidote used to rescue normal cells.

text: The SH2 domain found in many signaling proteins binds specifically to:
options: SH3 domains on other signaling proteins
Phosphorylated tyrosine residues (pTyr) on activated receptor tyrosine kinases and adaptor proteins
GTP-bound Ras family small G proteins
Lipid second messengers like PIP3
explanation: SH2 (Src homology 2) domains are ~100-residue modular protein domains that specifically recognize phosphorylated tyrosine (pTyr) residues in the context of specific surrounding amino acids. This specificity enables the assembly of signaling complexes downstream of receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs). When an RTK (e.g., EGFR, PDGFR) autophosphorylates its tyrosine residues, SH2-containing proteins (Grb2, PI3K p85, PLCγ, SHP2, Src) dock at specific pTyr sites → activating distinct downstream pathways. SH3 domains bind proline-rich PXXP motifs. PH domains bind PIP3 and other phosphoinositides. WD40, RING, and BRCT domains have other functions. The SH2-pTyr interaction is the molecular basis for RTK signal specificity.

text: Which of the following correctly describes the role of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress response (unfolded protein response, UPR)?
options: ER stress response is activated by nutrient excess and promotes anabolism

The UPR is triggered when misfolded proteins accumulate in the ER lumen; sensors (IRE1, PERK, ATF6) activate downstream pathways to: reduce new protein translation, upregulate ER chaperones (BiP/GRP78), and if unresolved, trigger apoptosis
The UPR exports misfolded proteins to the Golgi for degradation
ER stress activates the proteasome pathway in the cytoplasm directly
explanation: The UPR (unfolded protein response) is activated when ER chaperone BiP/GRP78 is titrated away by unfolded proteins, freeing three ER transmembrane sensors: (1) PERK (eIF2α kinase): globally reduces translation, activates ATF4 for stress-gene transcription; (2) IRE1 (endoribonuclease): splices XBP1 mRNA → active XBP1s transcription factor → upregulates ERAD (ER-associated degradation) and chaperones; (3) ATF6 (transcription factor): cleaved in Golgi → upregulates UPR target genes. If unresolved, sustained UPR promotes apoptosis via CHOP. ERAD retrotranslocates misfolded proteins to the cytoplasm for proteasomal degradation. UPR is relevant in multiple myeloma (high Ig production stress), diabetes (β-cell failure), and neurodegeneration.

text: In which phase of the cell cycle does DNA synthesis occur, and what kinase complex drives the G1/S transition?
options: G2 phase; CDK1-Cyclin B drives G1/S
S phase; Cyclin E-CDK2 drives the G1/S transition by phosphorylating Rb (retinoblastoma protein), releasing E2F transcription factors to activate S-phase genes
M phase; CDK4-Cyclin D drives S phase entry
G1 phase; CDK2-Cyclin A promotes DNA replication
explanation: Cell cycle: G1 (growth) → S (DNA synthesis) → G2 (growth/repair) → M (mitosis). The G1/S restriction point is controlled by: (1) CDK4/6-Cyclin D (induced by mitogens): phosphorylates Rb → partial E2F release; (2) CDK2-Cyclin E: further phosphorylates Rb → hyperphosphorylation → complete E2F release → transcription of S-phase genes (DNA polymerases, thymidylate synthase, PCNA). CDK inhibitors (p21, p27, p16/INK4a) halt this process. CDK1-Cyclin B ('MPF') drives G2/M. CDK2-Cyclin A drives S phase progression and DNA replication. Rb is a classic tumor suppressor — lost in retinoblastoma and many cancers; p16 loss also bypasses the G1/S checkpoint.

text: A mutation in the CFTR gene results in misfolding of the protein and its retention in the ER, where it is degraded by ERAD. This is the mechanism underlying which disease?
options: Marfan syndrome
Cystic fibrosis (CF) — the most common CF mutation ΔF508 causes CFTR misfolding and ER retention; only a small amount of functional CFTR reaches the apical membrane of epithelial cells
Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency
Phenylketonuria
explanation: CFTR is a cAMP-regulated chloride channel on the apical membrane of epithelial cells (airways, pancreatic ducts, sweat glands, intestine, vas deferens). ΔF508 (deletion of phenylalanine at position 508) is the most common CF mutation (~70% of CF alleles): it causes temperature-sensitive misfolding → BiP retention in ER → ERAD degradation → no CFTR at cell surface → no Cl⁻ secretion → thick, viscous mucus in lungs (chronic infection, bronchiectasis), pancreatic insufficiency, meconium ileus, male infertility. CFTR modulators: ivacaftor (potentiator, opens the gate); lumacaftor/tezacaftor (correctors, help ΔF508-CFTR reach the membrane); trikafta (elexacaftor+tezacaftor+ivacaftor) — highly effective triple therapy.

text: Steroid hormones exert their effects primarily through which molecular mechanism?
options: Binding to G protein-coupled receptors on the plasma membrane, activating cAMP signaling
Diffusing through the plasma membrane and binding intracellular (nuclear) receptors that act as ligand-activated transcription factors, directly regulating gene expression
Binding to receptor tyrosine kinases on the cell surface
Activating ion channels on the plasma membrane directly
explanation: Steroid hormones (cortisol, estrogen, testosterone, progesterone, aldosterone, vitamin D, thyroid hormone) are lipid-soluble and diffuse through the plasma membrane. They bind intracellular nuclear receptors (NRs), which are ligand-activated transcription factors. Mechanism: unbound NRs are often in the cytoplasm complexed with heat shock proteins (HSPs). Hormone binding causes HSP dissociation → receptor dimerization → nuclear translocation → binding to specific DNA hormone response elements (HREs) → recruitment of co-activators or co-repressors → modulation of target gene transcription. This mechanism is slow (hours) compared to membrane receptor signaling (seconds-minutes). Some steroids also have rapid non-genomic effects via membrane-associated receptors.

text: A researcher treats cells with cycloheximide, which inhibits the 80S ribosome. Which process would be most directly impaired?
options: Transcription of mRNA in the nucleus
Cytoplasmic translation of mRNA into protein in eukaryotic cells — the peptidyl transferase activity of the 60S subunit is blocked
Mitochondrial translation of mtDNA-encoded proteins
Post-translational modification in the Golgi
explanation: Cycloheximide inhibits the peptidyl transferase activity of the 80S (eukaryotic) ribosome — specifically the 60S subunit — blocking translocation and thus protein synthesis in the cytoplasm. It does NOT inhibit: (1) prokaryotic ribosomes (70S) — used to distinguish eukaryotic vs. prokaryotic translation; (2) mitochondrial ribosomes (also 70S-like, inhibited by chloramphenicol, not cycloheximide). This is why cycloheximide is used experimentally to: study protein stability (pulse-chase), measure protein degradation rates, and inhibit new protein synthesis. Clinically relevant: antibiotics targeting 70S ribosomes (aminoglycosides, tetracyclines, chloramphenicol, macrolides, clindamycin) are selective for bacteria.

text: Nitric oxide (NO) is synthesized by endothelial cells and causes vascular smooth muscle relaxation. What is the molecular mechanism?
options: NO binds to beta-adrenergic receptors, activating cAMP/PKA and phosphorylating myosin light chain kinase

NO diffuses into smooth muscle cells and binds soluble guanylyl cyclase (sGC), stimulating conversion of GTP to cGMP, which activates PKG → phosphorylates and inactivates MLCK → decreased myosin phosphorylation → vasodilation
NO activates voltage-gated calcium channels, increasing intracellular Ca²+ in smooth muscle
NO directly depolymerizes actin filaments in vascular smooth muscle
explanation: NO (from eNOS, using L-arginine + O2 + NADPH) is a gaseous signaling molecule that diffuses across membranes. In vascular smooth muscle: NO binds the heme iron of soluble guanylyl cyclase (sGC) → conformational change → sGC converts GTP → cGMP → cGMP activates PKG (protein kinase G) → PKG phosphorylates: myosin light chain phosphatase (activates it, dephosphorylates myosin) and myosin light chain kinase (inhibits it) → less myosin phosphorylation → less actin-myosin cross-bridge cycling → smooth muscle relaxation → vasodilation. Nitroglycerin and sildenafil (PDE5 inhibitor, prevents cGMP breakdown) exploit this pathway. ACh → eNOS → NO → vasodilation (endothelium-dependent relaxation).

text: Which of the following correctly explains why saturated fatty acids are solid at room temperature while unsaturated fatty acids are liquid?
options: Saturated fatty acids have more double bonds, increasing their melting points

Saturated fatty acids have no double bonds and adopt fully extended, straight-chain conformations that pack tightly together, increasing van der Waals interactions and raising melting point; unsaturated fatty acids have cis double bonds that introduce kinks, preventing tight packing and lowering melting point
The length of the carbon chain determines physical state, not saturation
Unsaturated fatty acids have charged groups that repel each other
explanation: Saturated fatty acids (no double bonds) adopt an extended zigzag (all-trans) conformation that allows tight, parallel packing between chains → strong van der Waals interactions → high melting point → solid at room temperature (butter, lard, coconut oil). Unsaturated fatty acids have cis double bonds that introduce rigid, ~30° kinks in the chain → disrupt tight packing → weaker van der Waals → lower melting point → liquid at room temperature (olive oil, fish oil). Trans fatty acids (straight-chain like saturated fats) also pack tightly and raise melting point → solid at room temperature, similar cardiovascular risk to saturated fats. Membrane fluidity is maintained by unsaturated/short-chain fatty acids; cholesterol buffers fluidity changes.

text: Which enzyme is responsible for the initial step of nitrogen fixation in Rhizobium bacteria, and what is the product?
options: Glutamine synthetase, which fixes atmospheric N2 into glutamine
Nitrogenase complex, which reduces atmospheric N2 to NH3 (ammonia) — this requires 16 ATP and is inhibited by oxygen
Nitrate reductase, which converts NO3⁻ to NO2⁻
Glutamate dehydrogenase, which incorporates NH4+ into glutamate
explanation: Biological nitrogen fixation: N₂ + 8H+ + 8e⁻ + 16ATP → 2NH₃ + H₂ + 16ADP + 16Pi. Catalyzed by the nitrogenase complex (dinitrogenase reductase + dinitrogenase), which contains iron-sulfur clusters and a MoFe cofactor. Critical features: (1) Strongly inhibited by O₂ (destroys the Fe-S clusters) — Rhizobium in root nodules is protected by leghemoglobin (encoded by both plant and bacterium) which scavenges O₂; (2) Extremely ATP-intensive; (3) H₂ is always released as a byproduct. Products NH₃ → glutamine (via glutamine synthetase) → glutamate (via glutamate synthase/GOGAT) → all other N-containing biomolecules. This is the primary entry point of atmospheric N into the biosphere.

text: A patient undergoes a bone marrow transplant and develops graft-versus-host disease (GVHD). What is the underlying immunological mechanism?
options: The patient's immune system rejects the transplanted marrow
Donor T cells present in the transplant recognize host (recipient) MHC molecules as foreign and mount an immune attack against host tissues (GI tract, skin, liver)
NK cells in the graft attack cancer cells remaining in the patient
The patient lacks MHC molecules and cannot activate transplanted T cells
explanation: GVHD occurs when immunocompetent donor T cells (in the graft) recognize the recipient's MHC molecules as foreign (alloreactive T cells): (1) Direct allorecognition: donor T cells bind recipient APCs displaying recipient's allogeneic MHC directly; (2) Indirect allorecognition: donor T cells recognize recipient-derived peptides presented by donor MHC on donor APCs. Activated donor T cells attack recipient tissues: skin (rash), GI tract (diarrhea, nausea), liver (elevated LFTs), lungs. Acute GVHD (<100 days) and chronic GVHD are distinct entities. Prevention: HLA matching, T cell depletion of graft, calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine). Note: graft-versus-leukemia (GVL) effect is beneficial — donor T cells also kill residual leukemic cells.

text: The hormone glucagon primarily acts on the liver to raise blood glucose. Which intracellular signaling pathway does it activate?
options: PI3K-Akt pathway, inhibiting glycogen synthase kinase

Gs-adenylyl cyclase-cAMP-PKA pathway: glucagon binds Gs-coupled GPCR → adenylyl cyclase → ↑cAMP → PKA activation → phosphorylates and activates glycogen phosphorylase kinase → activates glycogen phosphorylase → glycogenolysis; also inhibits glycogen synthase and activates gluconeogenesis
Gq-PLCβ-IP3-DAG-PKC pathway
MAPK/ERK pathway, activating transcription of gluconeogenic enzymes
explanation: Glucagon (from pancreatic α-cells) is the primary counter-regulatory hormone to insulin. Its receptor is a Gs-coupled GPCR: glucagon → Gs → adenylyl cyclase activation → ↑cAMP → PKA activation. PKA phosphorylates: (1) Phosphorylase kinase → phosphorylates/activates glycogen phosphorylase → glycogenolysis (glucose-1-P → glucose-6-P → glucose); (2) Glycogen synthase → inactivates glycogen synthesis; (3) PFK-2/FBPase-2 (bifunctional enzyme) → reduces F-2,6-BP → relieves allosteric activation of PFK-1 → decreases glycolysis; (4) Transcription factors (CREB) → upregulate PEPCK and G6Pase for gluconeogenesis. Net hepatic effect: ↑glycogenolysis, ↓glycolysis, ↑gluconeogenesis → ↑blood glucose.

text: In a patient with hemophilia A, what is the primary coagulation defect?
options: Deficiency of von Willebrand factor
Deficiency of Factor VIII, which is required as a cofactor for Factor IXa to activate Factor X in the intrinsic pathway of the coagulation cascade
Deficiency of Factor VII in the extrinsic pathway
Deficiency of platelet glycoprotein IIb/IIIa
explanation: Hemophilia A: X-linked recessive deficiency of Factor VIII (FVIII). In the coagulation cascade: intrinsic pathway — contact activation (XII → XIa → IXa); FVIII is a cofactor that dramatically accelerates Factor IXa's activation of Factor X (10,000-fold). Without FVIII: the IXa-VIIIa complex ('tenase complex') cannot form → Factor Xa is not efficiently generated → insufficient thrombin and fibrin clot. Lab findings: prolonged aPTT (tests intrinsic + common pathway), normal PT (extrinsic pathway intact), normal bleeding time (primary hemostasis intact — platelets and vWF are normal). Treatment: recombinant FVIII or emicizumab (bispecific antibody mimicking FVIII function). Hemophilia B: Factor IX deficiency (same lab picture).

text: Which statement correctly describes the central dogma of molecular biology as understood today?
options: DNA → RNA → Protein, with no known exceptions

The primary flow is DNA → RNA → Protein, but exceptions exist: reverse transcriptase copies RNA back to DNA (retroviruses), RNA can replicate RNA (RNA viruses), and prions demonstrate protein conformation change without nucleic acid
Proteins can directly encode information and synthesize new DNA sequences
RNA is never translated back to DNA under any circumstances
explanation: Crick's central dogma (1958): information flows DNA → DNA (replication), DNA → RNA (transcription), RNA → Protein (translation). Exceptions now known: (1) RNA → DNA: reverse transcriptase (HIV, retroviruses) — used in HIV replication and cDNA library construction; (2) RNA → RNA: RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RNA viruses like poliovirus, influenza) — basis for viral genome replication; (3) Prion hypothesis: abnormal protein conformation (PrPsc) propagates by converting normal PrPc — information encoded in protein structure, not sequence; no nucleic acid involved. The dogma holds that amino acid sequences cannot be reverse-translated to nucleotide sequences — this remains unchallenged.

text: A 35-year-old develops a pulmonary embolism. Anticoagulation with heparin is initiated. What is the primary mechanism of heparin's anticoagulant effect?
options: Heparin directly inhibits thrombin and Factor Xa by occupying their active sites
Heparin binds and activates antithrombin III (ATIII), increasing its inhibitory activity ~1000-fold against thrombin (IIa) and Factor Xa, as well as IXa, XIa, and XIIa
Heparin prevents vitamin K-dependent carboxylation of coagulation factors
Heparin inhibits platelet aggregation by blocking thromboxane A2 synthesis
explanation: Heparin (unfractionated) is a glycosaminoglycan that binds antithrombin III (ATIII) via a specific pentasaccharide sequence, causing conformational change → ATIII becomes a rapid inhibitor of thrombin (IIa) and other serine proteases (Xa, IXa, XIa, XIIa). For thrombin inhibition, heparin must also bind thrombin simultaneously (ternary complex) — requires ≥18 saccharides. LMWH (low molecular weight heparin: enoxaparin) predominantly inhibits Factor Xa (anti-Xa > anti-IIa). Fondaparinux: synthetic pentasaccharide, pure Factor Xa inhibitor via ATIII. Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase → depletes γ-carboxylation of factors II, VII, IX, X. Direct oral anticoagulants (apixaban, rivaroxaban) directly inhibit Xa or IIa (dabigatran).

text: A researcher studying protein folding finds that a small protein folds spontaneously in a test tube without any chaperones. This observation is consistent with which principle?
options: The information for the final 3D structure is NOT encoded in the primary amino acid sequence

Anfinsen's principle: the native (biologically active) 3D structure of a protein is the thermodynamically most stable conformation under physiological conditions, and all information needed to reach this state is encoded in the primary sequence
Chaperones are required for all protein folding in vivo
Proteins fold along a single defined pathway determined by their mRNA structure
explanation: Anfinsen's ribonuclease experiment (Nobel Prize 1972): RNase A was denatured with urea (unfolded) and reduced with β-mercaptoethanol (broke disulfide bonds) → completely inactive. When renaturation conditions were restored (remove denaturants), RNase A spontaneously refolded to its native structure and regained full enzymatic activity. This demonstrated that all information for 3D structure is encoded in the primary amino acid sequence and that the native state is the thermodynamic minimum. Chaperones prevent misfolding and aggregation in vivo (crowded cellular environment) but are not required for the thermodynamic driving force — they increase the kinetic efficiency and fidelity of folding.

text: In which situation does the kidney secrete potassium (K+) into the tubular lumen?
options: During hypokalemia, to conserve potassium

In the principal cells of the cortical collecting duct, when aldosterone is high — aldosterone upregulates Na+/K+-ATPase and apical K+ channels (ROMK), driving K+ secretion in exchange for Na+ reabsorption
In the loop of Henle, as the primary site of K+ regulation
When the patient is acidotic, K+ is secreted in exchange for H+
explanation: K+ homeostasis: most K+ is reabsorbed in the proximal tubule (~67%) and thick ascending limb (~25%). Fine-tuning occurs in the cortical collecting duct (CCD). Principal cells: aldosterone → binds mineralocorticoid receptor → upregulates ENaC (apical Na+ channel) and Na+/K+-ATPase (basolateral) → Na+ reabsorption creates lumen electronegativity → driving force for K+ secretion via ROMK channels. High aldosterone (hyperaldosteronism) → hypokalemia. RAAS activation also includes ADH and volume status. Acidosis: H+ competes with K+ for secretion → hyperkalemia. Alkalosis → hypokalemia. K+-sparing diuretics: spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist), amiloride (blocks ENaC). Loop diuretics also increase K+ loss by increasing CCD flow rate.

text: A patient with type 1 diabetes has blood glucose of 400 mg/dL and produces large amounts of ketone bodies. Why does this occur despite glucose being available?
options: Type 1 diabetes reduces GLUT2 transporter expression in the liver

Absolute insulin deficiency prevents glucose uptake by muscle and adipose tissue; the liver interprets this as a fasting state, activating β-oxidation and ketogenesis from free fatty acids released by unregulated lipolysis in adipose tissue
Excess glucose directly activates glucagon receptors, stimulating ketogenesis
The kidneys fail to filter glucose and divert it to ketone synthesis
explanation: In type 1 diabetes (absolute insulin deficiency): (1) Glucose cannot enter muscle or adipose tissue (GLUT4 translocation requires insulin) → hyperglycemia; (2) Adipocytes: without insulin, hormone-sensitive lipase is unchecked → massive lipolysis → free fatty acids released into blood; (3) Liver: low insulin + high glucagon → oxaloacetate used for gluconeogenesis rather than condensing with acetyl-CoA; (4) Hepatic β-oxidation of FFAs generates excess acetyl-CoA that cannot enter TCA (insufficient OAA) → ketogenesis (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate) → ketoacidosis. The liver 'thinks' it's starving because insulin is absent. The paradox: hyperglycemia + starvation metabolism coexist. Treatment: insulin (shuts down lipolysis, activates GLUT4, inhibits glucagon).

text: In gel electrophoresis with SDS-PAGE, proteins are separated based on:
options: Charge and shape — proteins with more negative charges migrate faster toward the anode
Molecular weight only — SDS denatures proteins and coats them with uniform negative charge proportional to mass; smaller proteins migrate faster through the polyacrylamide gel matrix
Isoelectric point — proteins at their pI do not migrate
Protein-protein interactions — proteins in the same complex migrate together
explanation: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate) is an anionic detergent that: (1) Denatures proteins (disrupts non-covalent interactions) and (2) Binds at ~1.4 g SDS per g protein, coating the protein with uniform negative charge proportional to its mass. This eliminates the effect of intrinsic charge and shape. Under an electric field, all proteins migrate toward the anode (positive electrode), but smaller proteins pass through the polyacrylamide matrix more easily than larger ones → separation by molecular weight. The log(MW) vs. migration distance is linear → MW can be determined using a protein ladder. Note: disulfide bonds must be reduced (β-mercaptoethanol or DTT) for complete denaturation and accurate sizing of multi-chain proteins.
